companies.name: Verizon
companies.analystNotes: Reference case for the index. Years of CEI 100 scores, Pride sponsorships, and an ERG program were unwound in May 2025 when Verizon pledged to the FCC to end DEI policies the day before its $20B Frontier acquisition was approved. CEO Vestberg's 2020 'fiercely committed to diversity' statement was deleted from the web.

rationale.verdict: A longtime self-declared ally that stripped its DEI commitments in May 2025 to win FCC approval of its Frontier acquisition.
rationale.driversUp: Annual rainbow branding and Pride statements; sponsorship of Pride events; a funded LGBTQ+ employee resource group; CEI score of 100 as recently as 2024.
rationale.driversDown: In May 2025 Verizon told the FCC it would end DEI policies, scrub 'diversity, equity and inclusion' from training and public materials, and cease related sponsorships; the FCC approved its $20B Frontier deal the next day (NPR, The Intercept). CEO Hans Vestberg's 2020 statement that Verizon was 'fiercely committed to diversity and inclusion' was removed from its site (Popular Information).
rationale.decisiveFactor: The May 2025 reversal under direct regulatory pressure — support evaporated exactly when it carried a cost, proving it was conditional.

companies.name: AT&T
companies.analystNotes: Home of LEAGUE, one of the first LGBTQ+ ERGs in corporate America (1987), and a decade of CEI 100 scores. Quit the CEI and cut funding to LGBTQ+ groups, then formally pledged to the FCC in Dec 2025 to end DEI programs as it sought approval of its $1B+ spectrum/UScellular transactions. Documented anti-LGBTQ+ political giving lowers the score (-30); see Political_Donations sheet.

rationale.verdict: Decades of pioneering LGBTQ+ workplace inclusion ended with a written pledge to the FCC to abandon DEI.
rationale.driversUp: LEAGUE at AT&T, founded 1987, is among the oldest LGBTQ+ ERGs in the US; years of CEI 100 scores; long-running Pride event sponsorships (e.g., Dallas Pride) and rainbow campaign branding.
rationale.driversDown: Stopped participating in HRC's Corporate Equality Index and ceased funding some LGBTQ+ organizations; in a Dec 1, 2025 FCC filing committed to ending DEI programs company-wide, with hiring and advancement framed as purely 'merit-based' (CNN, ESG Dive). Campaign-finance records additionally document contributions to anti-LGBTQ+ politicians/committees (see Political_Donations sheet; Data for Progress 2019-22 / Popular Information 2022-24).
rationale.decisiveFactor: The December 2025 FCC letter — a formal, written renunciation of DEI made to secure deal approval.

rationale.decisiveFactor: The July 2025 written renunciation of DEI as the explicit price of regulatory approval.

rationale.verdict: A quiet structural participant mis-read as harmful: Charter holds a 90 HRC CEI (5/5 nondiscrimination, 45/50 benefits) but discloses little and has faced discrimination allegations; a 2021 anti-LGBTQ political-donation flag drags the score.

rationale.driversDown: Documented corporate political donations to anti-LGBTQ legislators (Data for Progress 'Accountable Allies', 2021/22); DEI disclosure lags peers; past discrimination allegations on sexual orientation/gender.
rationale.decisiveFactor: The political-donation flag (−30) is the single largest drag; the strong but low-profile CEI substance pulls it back into Ally range. Lighter public engagement keeps confidence moderate.

companies.name: Apple
companies.analystNotes: The strongest large-cap record in the index: out CEO since 2014, annual Pride Edition products, SF Pride sponsorship, and a 97.3% shareholder vote rejecting an anti-DEI proposal in Feb 2025. Maintained its inclusion language while peers scrubbed theirs.

rationale.verdict: A sustained, structural ally that publicly held its DEI position through the 2025 backlash.
rationale.driversUp: Tim Cook has been the most prominent openly gay CEO in America since 2014; annual Apple Watch Pride Edition bands and watch faces since 2016; SF Pride sponsorship; explicit inclusion commitments still on apple.com; shareholders rejected an anti-DEI proposal 97.3% in Feb 2025 with the board's backing; CEI 100.
rationale.driversDown: Operates in countries that criminalize homosexuality without comparable advocacy there — a limit on, though not a reversal of, its commitments. No post-2025 retreat documented.
rationale.decisiveFactor: Holding the line in February 2025: Apple urged shareholders to reject the anti-DEI proposal while peers capitulated preemptively.

companies.analystNotes: Kept benefits and ERGs but ended diversity hiring targets (Feb 2025, citing federal contractor status), scrubbed DEI from its 10-K and AI pages, and deleted Pride Month from Google Calendar defaults. A partial, pressure-driven hollowing-out of a once-loud ally.

rationale.verdict: A formerly loud ally that quietly dismantled the public scaffolding of its support while keeping internal benefits.
rationale.driversUp: Two decades of SF Pride sponsorship and employee contingents; funded LGBTQ+ ERGs; early adopter of trans-inclusive healthcare (2011-era gold standard); CEI 100 streak.
rationale.driversDown: Ended diversity hiring goals in Feb 2025 citing Trump-administration contractor rules (WSJ/Axios); removed Pride Month and other observances from Google Calendar; scrubbed 'diversity' from its 10-K (8 mentions in 2023 to zero) and from its Responsible AI page; retitled its Chief Diversity Officer role.
rationale.decisiveFactor: The February 2025 decision to end diversity hiring targets under federal pressure — the pivot from advocacy to compliance.

companies.analystNotes: The most aggressive Big Tech reversal: killed DEI programs days before the 2025 inauguration and rewrote hate-speech rules to explicitly permit calling LGBTQ+ people mentally ill. GLAAD and HRC condemned the changes; trans/nonbinary Messenger themes quietly vanished.

rationale.verdict: Preemptive capitulation: Meta dismantled DEI and rewrote its rules to permit anti-LGBTQ+ abuse before Trump took office.
rationale.driversUp: Years of rainbow branding and Pride features across Facebook/Instagram; funded LGBTQ+ ERG; trans-inclusive benefits; CEI 100 history.
rationale.driversDown: Jan 2025: ended DEI team, representation goals and supplier diversity (Axios memo); revised Hateful Conduct policy to allow 'allegations of mental illness or abnormality' based on gender or sexual orientation — condemned by GLAAD; removed trans and nonbinary Messenger themes; ended fact-checking that curbed anti-LGBTQ+ hate (LGBTQ Nation).
rationale.decisiveFactor: The January 2025 hate-speech carve-out — Meta didn't just retreat from support, it re-engineered its platform rules in ways that target LGBTQ+ users.

companies.name: Microsoft
companies.analystNotes: Held its DEI position publicly through 2025 — kept its inclusion report, CEI participation, Pride campaigns and ERG funding. The July 2024 DEI-team layoff drew criticism but was not followed by policy retreat. One of the steadiest large-cap records.

rationale.verdict: A steady structural ally that kept its commitments intact through the backlash years.
rationale.driversUp: Added sexual orientation to its nondiscrimination policy in 1989, among the first majors; funded GLEAM ERG; trans-inclusive benefits; annual Xbox/Microsoft Pride campaigns with merchandise and donations; 2024 Global Diversity & Inclusion report publicly reaffirmed by CEO Nadella; CEI 100 streak maintained.
rationale.driversDown: Laid off an internal DEI team in July 2024 citing 'changing business needs' (Business Insider memo) — a warning sign, though public commitments and the inclusion report continued.
rationale.decisiveFactor: Continuity: while peers scrubbed pages and quit the CEI, Microsoft kept reporting and kept its programs.

rationale.decisiveFactor: Erasing the word 'transgender' from its public positions — a targeted scrubbing of the most vulnerable group while Pride merch stayed on sale.

rationale.decisiveFactor: The March 2025 10-K rewrite: values that defined the brand were quietly edited out under political pressure, though programs largely persist.

companies.name: Booz Allen Hamilton
companies.analystNotes: Scrapped DEI programs in Feb 2025 citing federal contract risk, then dropped its WorldPride 2025 sponsorship — the most-cited single Pride withdrawal of the season.

rationale.verdict: The WorldPride defection: a marquee sponsorship abandoned weeks after DEI was scrapped for contract security.
rationale.driversUp: Prior WorldPride/Capital Pride sponsorship; funded LGBTQ+ ERG (GLOBE+); CEI 100 record; inclusive benefits.
rationale.driversDown: Feb 2025: scrapped DEI programs, with its Chief People Officer telling staff that without changes 'we could be ineligible for contracts with the federal government' (Bloomberg); then dropped its WorldPride 2025 sponsorship in Washington DC (Advocate).
rationale.decisiveFactor: Dropping WorldPride in its hometown — the highest-visibility Pride withdrawal of 2025.
